item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is almost identical to the stand "survivor" from JoJo's Bizarre Adventure. Right down to the idea that the sufferer has a warped sense of human behavior because of its constant effect.

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded: Noting that a recent edit was reverted for introducing unnecessary content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh wow… I just read the original version… Man, that was horrible. The current version is much improved. Gave a +1 before, but it's even more deserved for salvaging what it was before. Experiment logs are good too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I up voted this because of the Afghanistan reference, but re-reading this, it reminds me too much of 053. Even without the comparison, this is still pretty dull.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only interesting part is the bit at the end, the actual article is blagh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is it necessary for this woman to have a 10 m (~33 ft) tall room? Does her "conflict aura" extend through walls? If so shouldn't her containment cell be at least 20 m thick on all sides to prevent people in the halls from becoming agitated? Just saying

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. Sure, there shouldn't be a whole lot of emphasis on it, but personalities are kinda par for the course with sapient beings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um.. you noticed that comment is four years old?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude, we're the SCP Foundation. "SCP" stands for "Secure, Contain, Protect", not "Destroy, Destroy, Destroy". We squirrel stuff away because we can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i'm having a hard time believing a soviet soldier would describe afghanistan as "the middle east." a: it's not, it's central asia, and b: surely there's a (translated) russian term that would make more sense for him to use - the middle east is a british invention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My bad, the math is right on part 1, as the soviets invaded Afghanistan in '79.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, yes. How dare a living being have a personality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yet another reason I hate setting actual years down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see how making her communicative gives her a "personality". I mean, certainly it implies she's not a comatose robot, but …

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I find writing articles as if they were written in, say, 2020, pretty retarded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I assumed that it was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tombones -
1. Don't know why I didn't think of that. Will institute changes.
2. Yeah, see the expt. log.
Full Hazard-
1. The interview took place in 1991. The soldier was talking about his experiences during the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan.
2. You're correct, but: In universe answer - she's non-responsive or tests have been inconclusive. Out of universe answer - suspension of disbelief to make her more mysterious/detached. Humanoid SCPs with personalities piss me off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why do people always assume that the SCP series is set in the present day?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two things that bother me:
1: If the girl is 8-9 in 1991, and roughly 40 now, either the SCP universe is set in 2020, or somebody screwed up on the math.
2: As there is no indication that the area of effect of this SCP extending beyond 15 meters, couldn't it be possible to get an interview/psych exam with her by use of a telephone, or even just shouting?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: err, I hadn't noticed the experiment log. That answers my second question, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Didn't read the original, but I like how it reads now. The concept is pretty straightforward, but the idea that this woman has only ever seen other humans behaving irrationally violently toward each other is pretty unnerving. The eyewitness report adds to that, and I always find it interesting when the local population establishes their own "containment procedures" before the Foundation even gets involved.

Some questions I'm left with-

-Wouldn't placing her containment cell in a suitably deep pit and using a standard dumbwaiter system for food/message delivery be a more effective use of space and resources than building a gymnasium-sized chamber around the cell and setting up a fancy conveyer belt system?
-What happens if only one person is inside her "area of effect"? Do they get into a violent argument with themselves? Do they attack the next person they see after leaving the area?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You may have saved this one, Sampi.

May.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll hold off on ym vote for now, and see what Joshua can do with this thing. You have permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll take the defib paddles to this one, with permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are we doing anything about this one? I recommend deletion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Contravoted. Containment procedure should be tailored to SCP effect, not the other way around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since this SCP is a sentient humanoid, I think the article could be improved by some interviews or any kind of communication with it. Any thoughts? How do you think she feels about her powers?

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Contravoted. This is an excellent SCP and its indestructibility is essential to its current containment procedures, which would otherwise be "SCP-114 is to be kept in a wooden box, buried at least six (6) feet underground…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too many industrictables

item.title: Re: Perhaps I don't quite get this, but...
item.content:encoded: I think the Bringer is Euclid because her nature is clear and her confinement simple: Lock her her up and leave her strictly alone.

item.title: Perhaps I don't quite get this, but...
item.content:encoded: Indestructible and could possibly lead to the extinction of mankind? Wouldn't that be Keter material?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit light on the actual details, but YES! Too few SCPs have reasons to keep the SCP around, or lack of documentation on destruction attempts.
